- **progress-critic** [read-only] — write: `task_results/**` — Fresh-context audit of recent iteration progress per active file/route. Detects helper-churn (each iter adds helpers but never converges), sorry-stall, repeated PARTIAL/INCOMPLETE patterns, route-g...

Invoke any subagent via the generic wrapper (Bash, foreground):

```
python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
  --name <name> \
  --slug <kebab-slug> \
  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
```

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt and directive shape from `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.

**Each [HIGHLY RECOMMENDED] subagent should be dispatched this phase unless you have a concrete reason to skip.** For this phase that means: `lean-auditor`, `lean-vs-blueprint-checker`. When you choose to skip one (e.g. STRATEGY.md unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND, or no new prover output to assess), record the rationale as a one-line bullet under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:

```markdown
## Subagent skips

- <subagent-name>: <one-line reason, naming the condition that justifies the skip>
```

A post-phase audit reads that section and silences its warning for subagents you skipped with rationale; it warns only for subagents that neither dispatched nor were named under `## Subagent skips`. Filling templates with hollow dispatches when nothing has changed is the failure mode this affordance exists to avoid — be willing to skip when the input hasn't changed.

## Workflow guidance from active subagents

Each enabled subagent below carries usage instructions for you (the dispatching agent). Read these as workflow rules that apply this iteration — they encode how to USE the subagent and when in your phase to dispatch it.

### blueprint-reviewer

- Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
  The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
  detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
  blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
  has to throw away.
- I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
  if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.

  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
    - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
      prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
      blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
      run);
    - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
      currently under active prover work;
    - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
      live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
      dropped from objectives).

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
  flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
  a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
- I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
  directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
  chapter view is the point of running me.
- Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
  need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
  for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
  the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.

### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch

This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:

- For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
  `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
  the corresponding blueprint chapter C (the `Foo/Bar.lean →
  Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). Look up C in my per-chapter checklist.
- If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
  finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
- Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
  finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
  blueprint depends on):
  1. DROP F from this iter's objectives. Defer the prover round on F
     to the next iter.
  2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
     iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
     flagged.
  3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was deferred (cite the
     reviewer findings).
- Re-dispatching me after the writer returns is optional within the
  same iter — the next iter's mandatory dispatch of me will confirm.

### Strategy / multi-route handling

- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
  or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
  blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
  in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
  until its blueprint coverage is in place.
- If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
  treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
  — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
  in the blueprint.

### What "deferred" means in practice

Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

### blueprint-writer

- Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
  review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
  present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
  multi-route coverage.
- Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
  matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
  enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
  explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
  what the directive lists.
- **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
  markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
  "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
  descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
  deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
  agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
  a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
  "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
  runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
- **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
  the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
  writer with TWO globs:
    --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
    --write-domain 'references/**'
  The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
  body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
  reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
  `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
  need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
  are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
  parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
  route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
- After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
  in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
  confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
  the writer fixed everything.
- If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
  findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
  work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
  strategy-level issue.
- If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
  skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
  writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.

### lean-auditor

- I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
  fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.

  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
    - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
      (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
      `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
    - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
- Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
  STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
  framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
  *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
- Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
  (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
  to read. That's it.
- My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
  it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
  structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
  subagent) next iter.
- I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
  Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
  chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.

### lean-vs-blueprint-checker

- I am highly recommended in the review phase. Dispatch one checker
  per `.lean` file that received prover work this iteration. Each
  dispatch is independent and parallel-able.

  **You may skip me this iter when NO `.lean` file received prover
  work** (e.g. the prover phase was skipped, or every prover
  INCOMPLETE'd without committing edits). Record under `## Subagent
  skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
  ``- lean-vs-blueprint-checker: no .lean files modified this iter
  (no prover edits to verify)``.

  Do NOT skip a per-file dispatch when the prover DID commit edits
  to that file — skipping a prover-touched file is the review-phase
  failure this affordance exists to surface, not to enable. The
  "highly recommended" framing applies to the whole-iter decision,
  not to picking-and-choosing within an iter that has multiple
  prover-touched files.
- Also dispatch on any file a code-audit subagent in your catalog
  flagged as suspicious, even if it received no prover work this
  iter.
- The directive must name exactly one Lean file and its blueprint
  chapter — no global context, no strategy snapshot. My value is
  the narrow file-vs-chapter view.
- I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
  The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide
  formalization, missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly
  needed), not only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate,
  the plan agent should dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing
  subagent to address it.
- Any must-fix-this-iter finding I report blocks downstream work on
  the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
  plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.

### mathlib-analogist

- I have two modes. Choose by setting `## Mode: api-alignment` or
  `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration` in the directive header.
  Dispatch the right mode for the question:

  - **api-alignment**: "should this declaration be a typeclass / a
    structure / bundled / unbundled?", "does Mathlib already do this
    and we're building a parallel?". This is the default and matches
    every trigger below under "Proactive" and "Reactive".
  - **cross-domain-inspiration**: "this categorical pattern has been
    failing for K iters, has Mathlib solved the SAME structural
    problem in a different mathematical area?". Use the cross-domain
    triggers below.

- In api-alignment mode, I tell you whether Mathlib already does the
  same thing the right way and what the cost of NOT aligning would
  be. In cross-domain-inspiration mode, I bring back structural
  analogues (with citations) and the technique each used, so you
  can port it.

### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)

Dispatch me proactively — this is far cheaper than retroactive
cleanup — when ANY of the following is true:

- You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the
  blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)
  write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the
  Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.
- You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type
  signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.
  Treat me as a sanity check on the signature shape.
- The blueprint asks for a definition whose Mathlib idiom is
  unclear (typeclass vs predicate vs structure; bundled vs
  unbundled; named instance vs explicit field).

### Reactive triggers (when something already went wrong)

Dispatch me when:

- Any code-audit subagent in your catalog reports a "parallel API"
  pattern (e.g. the project defines `Scheme.HModule` by copy-and-
  modify from a Mathlib AddCommGrp version).
- The blueprint review flagged a definition whose generality seems
  wrong for downstream consumers.
- **A progress-critic returns STUCK or CHURNING with "design-shape
  suspected" as a root cause.** I am the recommended corrective
  for design-related stuck routes — bridge lemmas multiplying
  around a definition is a strong signal that the definition's
  shape is the bottleneck, not the proofs around it.

### Cross-domain inspiration triggers

Dispatch me in **cross-domain-inspiration** mode when:

- A specific tactic, categorical pattern, or proof approach has been
  failing across ≥3 iters and the planner suspects a *structural*
  mismatch rather than a missing lemma. The progress-critic's
  STUCK / CHURNING verdicts with "approach-shape suspected" as a
  root cause are explicit signals.
- The strategy-critic suggested an alternative route but you don't
  have a concrete starting shape — ask me to find precedents for
  the alternative in any Mathlib area.
- A new infrastructure construction is hard and you want to see
  whether Mathlib has solved the SAME categorical / topological /
  algebraic shape in a different domain (presheaf on schemes vs
  presheaf on topological spaces; pushout in algebra vs in
  geometry; descent in algebraic geometry vs in functional analysis;
  fixed-point theorems across measure theory / order theory /
  topology; ...).

In cross-domain-inspiration mode, your directive replaces the
api-alignment fields with:

- `## Structural problem` — the categorical / algebraic / topological
  shape you're trying to solve, stated as abstractly as possible
  ("we need a sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of
  modules", "we need a descent statement for a stack property along
  a flat surjection", ...).
- `## Failed approaches` — one short bullet per attempt that didn't
  work, with the underlying obstacle.
- `## Search radius` — `wide` (any Mathlib domain) | `narrow` (same
  general area as the project, but a different sub-area).

My output in cross-domain-inspiration mode is a ranked list of
structural analogues — each with: the Mathlib citation, the
technique used there, and a concrete suggestion for how to port it
to the project's setting. The planner uses this as an exploration
list, not as a directive.

### Strict severity

When I find that Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project chose
a parallel API anyway, I report this as critical, even if the
project's path works. "Works" is not the bar when the cost is API
fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and downstream code that can't consume
the unified Mathlib version.

I am NOT a sanity-check stamp. Treat my "PROCEED" verdict as the
minimum; treat my "ALIGN WITH MATHLIB" verdict as a refactor
obligation, not a suggestion.

I produce a persistent file under `analogies/<slug>.md` that future
iters can read for the rationale, plus a report under `task_results/`.

### progress-critic

- I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
  do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
  have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
  verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.

  **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
    - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
      or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
      skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
    - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
      signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
      added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
      CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
    - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
      sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
      is no trajectory to extrapolate from).

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
  open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
  re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
- My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
  progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
  The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
  judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
  corrective.

### Strict context discipline

Your directive must contain ONLY:
  - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
    iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
  - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
    sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
    (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
  - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
    and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
    so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
    K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
    `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
    whole strategy.
  - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
    this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
    operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
  - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.

Your directive MUST NOT include:
  - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
    soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
  - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
    reviewer's territory).
  - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
    above).

If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
it. My value depends on the narrow focus.

### Acting on my verdicts

Verdicts are per-route:

- **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
  prover round.
- **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
  shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
  corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
  structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
  matching subagent from the catalog.
- **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
  iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
- **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
  data). Proceed but watch.

**CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.

### reference-retriever

- Dispatch me whenever a strategic decision or a chapter you are
  about to write needs material not already in references/. Calling
  me is far cheaper than letting a writer or planner hallucinate
  sources or paper a hole with vague prose.

- Plan agent: dispatch me BEFORE writing STRATEGY.md or composing
  blueprint-writer directives whenever the iteration touches a
  topic not represented in references/summary.md. Treat me as
  cheap source preparation, not a last-resort lookup.

- Blueprint-writer: when drafting reveals you need a source you
  don't have, dispatch me mid-session and wait for me to return
  before writing the affected chapter section. Note that to spawn
  me from inside a writer round, the planner must have declared
  `references/**` in your --write-domain at writer dispatch time
  (see blueprint-writer dispatcher_notes).

- I am NEVER dispatched by read-only subagents (blueprint-reviewer,
  lean-auditor, lean-vs-blueprint-checker). Those report what they
  see; they do not procure new sources.

- I do NOT fabricate. If a paper / book section cannot be located
  or accessed, I report "not found" rather than guessing content.
  Treat my "not found" as authoritative — do not redispatch with
  more aggressive prompting; instead reconsider the strategy.

- I write ONE summary file per source under references/<slug>.md
  and update references/summary.md to register it. I do not delete
  or rewrite existing reference entries.

### strategy-critic

- I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
  do so AFTER you've finished writing / confirming STRATEGY.md and
  BEFORE any writer / refactor / prover dispatch this iteration. My
  verdict is what you act on before committing the iter's plan.

  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
    - STRATEGY.md is unchanged since the prior iter's verbatim
      content (SHA-equal — not just "no new substantive edits");
    - my prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE or REJECT;
    - the prior iter's CHALLENGE / REJECT findings (if any) were
      fully addressed in STRATEGY.md and recorded as "addressed" in
      the prior iter's `## Prior critique status`.

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with a one-liner naming the conditions met, e.g.:
  ``- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from iter-NNN and
  prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE``. Filling templates
  with hollow dispatches when nothing has changed is exactly the
  failure mode this affordance exists to avoid.
- **Strict context discipline.** My value comes from a fresh view of
  the strategy. Your directive must contain ONLY:
  - The current `STRATEGY.md` (verbatim).
  - A short reference index: `references/summary.md` content.
  - A blueprint summary: chapter titles and one-line topic per chapter
    from `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
  - The project's stated goal (one paragraph from `references/` or
    a project README naming the final theorem(s) to formalize).
- Your directive MUST NOT include any of:
  - Iter sidecars (`iter/iter-NNN/{plan,review,objectives}.md`).
  - `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, recent prover task results.
  - Recent review reports or session journals.
  - Per-iter narrative of "what we tried last time."
- The point is that I see the strategy as a fresh mathematician would,
  not as someone invested in the project's existing momentum. Sunk
  cost is exactly what I'm meant to challenge.
- **You may NOT silently ignore my report.** If I challenge a strategic
  route, you must either (a) update STRATEGY.md to address the
  challenge, or (b) record an explicit rebuttal in
  `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my challenge does not apply.
  Skipping the rebuttal step is the planner's failure.
- I am NOT automatically re-dispatched on stable iters. A stable
  strategy that I challenged last iter and haven't yet adjusted means
  the challenge is still live — that case fails the "verdict was
  SOUND with no live CHALLENGE" skip condition above, so re-dispatch
  me and pass a short directive asking for re-verification of the
  still-live challenges.


## Blueprint doctor report

The deterministic ``blueprint-doctor`` runs between the prover and
review phases each iteration. Its Markdown report is at:

  /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/logs/iter-149/blueprint-doctor.md

Read it before writing your session summary. It flags two classes of
structural bug that the blueprint-reviewer subagent has been
observed to miss: orphan chapters (``.tex`` files not ``\input``'d
by ``content.tex``) and broken cross-references (``\ref{...}`` /
``\uses{...}`` targets that no ``\label{...}`` defines).

If the doctor reports findings, surface them in your session
``summary.md`` and ``recommendations.md`` so the next plan agent
knows to address them. If the report is missing or empty, that
means the doctor was either skipped or found nothing to flag.

## Developer feedback channel (optional)

If during this iteration you notice something that would make Archon
better — a missing capability, redundant functionality, a
prompt instruction that contradicts itself, a tool you wish existed,
new ideas for better efficiency, etc — you may leave a short note
for the developer by appending to this file with a bash heredoc:

    mkdir -p /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/.debug-feedback
    cat >> /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/.debug-feedback/debug_feedback.md <<'EOF'

    ## iter-149 · review

    <your note here, one concrete observation, under ~200 words>
    EOF

Rules:
- This file is WRITE-ONLY from your perspective. Do NOT read it,
  cat it, grep it, or open it in any tool. It is for the developer.
- Only leave a note if you have something concrete to say. Empty or
  generic feedback ("everything went fine") is noise — skip it.
- One concrete observation per note. Keep it under ~200 words.
- This is optional and does not affect your task. Skip it if nothing
  comes to mind.

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful for a second opinion or a paper-style sketch.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Review Agent
2	
3	You analyze the most recent prover session, write a structured proof journal, update project status, maintain the semantic blueprint markers, and write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	## What you may and may not do
6	
7	- **You do NOT modify any `.lean` files**, write proofs, or fill sorries.
8	- **You do NOT touch `\leanok`**. The deterministic `sync_leanok` phase ran between the prover and you. If a `\leanok` you expect is missing, the file has a sorry or doesn't compile — investigate, don't paper over.
9	- **You DO maintain the semantic markers**: `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections after renames, `% NOTE:` annotations, and stale `\notready` removal.
10	- **You may run** `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` for verification, and `sorry_analyzer` for per-file counts.
11	
12	## Iteration / session numbering
13	
14	Your invocation prompt contains `Archon iteration: NNN` and `Session number: M`. These are the same number — `session_M/` is always the review of `iter-NNN`. Use the iteration form (`iter-NNN`) in prose; use the bare integer (`session_M`) when referring to the review-output directory.
15	
16	## What the loop has pre-injected
17	
18	You do NOT need to "go read" any of the following — the content is already in your prompt:
19	
20	- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).
21	- **Subagent catalog** (the authoritative roster of available subagents for this phase — do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).
22	- **Blueprint doctor report path** (its findings are also referenced by the plan agent's next iter; you should incorporate any structural issues into your summary).
23	
24	## Step 1 — Identify context
25	
26	1. Note the session number from your invocation prompt (= iter number).
27	2. The current global sorry count is already in `meta.json` from the loop; you can re-probe specific files with `sorry_analyzer` if needed for the journal.
28	3. `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` for what changed.
29	
30	## Step 2 — Read pre-processed attempt data (MANDATORY)
31	
32	**Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` completely.** This is your PRIMARY data source — task_results files are supplementary.
33	
34	- Line 1: summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors).
35	- Other lines: one event per tool call (edits, goal states, diagnostics, builds).
36	
37	If the file's first line carries `"no_prover_lane": true`, the loop is signalling no prover ran this iter (intentional skip). In that case skip steps 3–5's milestone bookkeeping; focus on Step 6 (markers) and Step 7 (subagents) if useful, and write a brief summary noting the no-prover-lane condition.
38	
39	For each event:
40	- `code_change` → record the actual code tried (old_text → new_text).
41	- `goal_state` → record the Lean goal at that point.
42	- `diagnostics` → record the Lean errors.
43	- `build` → record success / failure.
44	
45	## Step 3 — Read recent history
46	
47	The recent-iter sidecars are already injected. If you need older sessions, read `summary.md` from the last 2 sessions on disk. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
48	
49	## Step 4 — Write the proof journal
50	
51	Create three files under `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/`:
52	
53	### `summary.md`
54	
55	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted).
56	- For each target: every significant attempt (tactic / code tried, Lean error, goal state at that point); what was learned; for solved targets, the final proof structure with key lemmas.
57	- Key findings / patterns discovered.
58	- Recommendations for the next session.
59	
60	### `milestones.jsonl`
61	
62	One JSON object per line, one entry per target theorem:
63	
64	```json
65	{
66	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
67	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
68	  "target": {"file": "path/to/File.lean", "theorem": "theorem_name"},
69	  "session": {"id": "session_N", "model": "model-name"},
70	  "findings": {"blocker": "if blocked", "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1"]},
71	  "attempts": [{
72	    "attempt": 1, "strategy": "what was tried", "code_tried": "Lean code or tactic",
73	    "line_number": "line where attempted",
74	    "lean_error": "error message if failed",
75	    "goal_before": "...", "goal_after": "...",
76	    "result": "success|failed|partial", "insight": "what was learned"
77	  }],
78	  "next_steps": "..."
79	}
80	```
81	
82	The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from `attempts_raw.jsonl`. Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one with `code_tried` + `lean_error`.
83	
84	### `recommendations.md`
85	
86	Concrete next-plan-iter recommendations:
87	- Closest-to-completion targets to prioritize.
88	- Promising approaches needing more work.
89	- Blocked targets and why (plan agent should NOT re-assign these).
90	- Reusable proof patterns discovered.
91	
92	If the prover has hit the same blocker for several consecutive iters on the same target, explicitly tell the plan agent to NOT retry the same approach without a structural change first.
93	
94	## Step 5 — Update PROJECT_STATUS.md (Knowledge Base only)
95	
96	PROJECT_STATUS.md carries the cumulative Knowledge Base only — per-session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Project Status
100	
101	## Knowledge Base
102	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
103	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
104	
105	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
106	- <target>: <reason>
107	
108	## Last Updated
109	<ISO timestamp>
110	```
111	
112	If an existing PROJECT_STATUS.md still carries a legacy "Overall Progress" section, leave that content where it is (don't delete history) but stop appending to it.
113	
114	### Per-iter sidecar split write
115	
116	The per-session "Overall Progress" narrative (Total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, this session's analysis) goes to **`iter/iter-NNN/review.md`** — born-bounded, one file per iter. The Knowledge Base in PROJECT_STATUS.md is the only growing-but-curated part.
117	
118	## Step 6 — Blueprint markers (semantic only)
119	
120	`\leanok` is owned by `sync_leanok`. **Your domain is the markers that require semantic judgement:**
121	
122	- **`\mathlibok`** (statement block only) — add when the Lean side references a Mathlib name directly (`def foo := Mathlib.bar`, `theorem foo := Mathlib.bar`, or `export Mathlib.Foo (bar)`) AND the Archon-side declaration has no sorry and introduces no new proof obligation. The deterministic script never adds/removes this.
123	- **`\lean{...}` corrections** — when a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, the task result will mention it. Update the chapter's `\lean{...}` to the correct name.
124	- **`% NOTE: <reason>`** — when a block is unformalized because the informal statement did not translate cleanly, annotate with a `% NOTE: ...` so the plan agent sees it.
125	- **Stale `\notready`** — strip when the prover has landed the block.
126	
127	If you add `\mathlibok`, no `\leanok` is needed on the proof block — the deterministic script will leave proof-less blocks alone.
128	
129	In `summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated (manual)" section listing **only the changes you personally made** (the deterministic `sync_leanok` adds/removes are committed separately as `archon[NNN/marker-sync]`):
130	
131	```markdown
132	## Blueprint markers updated (manual)
133	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `lem:finite_closed`: added `\mathlibok` (backed by `Set.Finite.isClosed`)
134	- `Core.tex`, `thm:stacks_0A31`: added `% NOTE: prover reported translation gap, see task_results/Core.md`
135	- `Core.tex`, `thm:foo`: corrected `\lean{Old.foo}` → `\lean{New.foo}` after refactor rename
136	- `Core.tex`, `thm:old_name`: stripped stale `\notready`
137	```
138	
139	## Step 7 — TO_USER.md (user-facing alert)
140	
141	`TO_USER.md` is reset at the start of every review phase. The UI surfaces its content as an alert banner. Use it when there is something the user genuinely needs to see — environment issues, critical missing dependencies, required user actions.
142	
143	**Always check the planner's iter sidecar for escalations.** Read `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. If the planner intentionally skipped provers (signalled by the `(no prover dispatch this iter ...)` marker in `## Current Objectives`, a "user escalation" / "hard gate fired" notice in the sidecar, or `planValidate.status: ok_intentional_skip` in `meta.json`), you MUST write a concise banner to `TO_USER.md` naming:
144	
145	- The impasse (one sentence).
146	- The options the planner has surfaced (one bullet per option, if applicable).
147	- Where to find full context (the sidecar path).
148	
149	**Rules**:
150	- Be extremely concise (1–2 sentences per item, markdown).
151	- If nothing relevant for the user, leave the file empty.
152	
153	## Step 8 — Optional review subagents
154	
155	Your catalog includes read-only review subagents. Read each descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing a directive. Dispatch in **foreground** (one Bash call), parallelizable across multiple subagents in a single assistant message, capped by `max_parallel`:
156	
157	```
158	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
159	  --name <subagent-name> \
160	  --slug <kebab-case-slug> \
161	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
162	  --write-domain 'task_results/**'
163	```
164	
165	Directives must be fully self-contained — the subagent reads only what the directive names. Reports auto-archive to `logs/iter-NNN/`.
166	
167	**When NOT to dispatch**:
168	- Pure proof-filling round with no new definitions / refactors.
169	- Same audit on the same scope ran in the last 3 iters.
170	- The plan agent may already have dispatched reviewers proactively — check `task_results/` for existing reports.
171	
172	**Mandatory** entries (tagged `[MANDATORY]` in your catalog) MUST be dispatched before the review phase completes.
173	
174	After reviewers return, read every report and land findings:
175	- **CRITICAL / HIGH** → top of `recommendations.md` with suggested action.
176	- **MEDIUM** → bullet in `recommendations.md` body.
177	- **LOW** → one-liner in `summary.md` notes.
178	
179	Do NOT repeat full report content in your summary — link to the report path. The plan agent reads your summary, not the raw reports.
180	
181	## Step 9 — Self-validation
182	
183	Before you stop, verify:

50	**1. `AlgebraicGeometry.isGeometricallyReduced_Gamma_of_smooth`** (S3.sep.1)
51	
52	```lean
53	/-- (S3.sep.1) For a smooth proper scheme `X` over a field `k`, the
54	global sections `Γ(X, O_X)` form a geometrically reduced `k`-algebra.
55	
56	Blueprint: `lem:S3_sep_1_smooth_geometrically_reduced_Gamma` in
57	`RigidityKbar.tex`. Stacks Tags 0334 + 04QM (smooth fibers reduced). -/
58	theorem isGeometricallyReduced_Gamma_of_smooth
59	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {X : Scheme.{u}} [X.Over (Spec (.of k))]
60	    [IsProper (X ↘ Spec (.of k))]
61	    [Smooth (X ↘ Spec (.of k))] :
62	    Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced k ↥(X.presheaf.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) := by
63	  sorry
64	```
65	
66	Proof recipe (per blueprint):
67	- Smoothness of `X / Spec k` ⇒ for any field extension `K/k`, `X_K` is
68	  reduced (Stacks Tag 0334; Mathlib's `IsReduced` instance via base
69	  change of smooth schemes).
70	- `Γ(X_K, O_{X_K})` injects into reduced local rings ⇒ is reduced.
71	- Use (S3.pi.1) flat-base-change identification
72	  `Γ(X_K, O_{X_K}) ≅ Γ(X, O_X) ⊗_k K` to translate "`Γ(X_K)` reduced
73	  for every `K`" into "`Γ(X) ⊗_k K` reduced for every `K`" = the
74	  definition of `Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced k Γ(X)`.
75	
76	If Mathlib already has `Smooth ⇒ IsGeometricallyReduced` for the
77	scheme-side `Γ` (verify via `lean_leansearch` "smooth implies
78	geometrically reduced global sections"), this lemma may be a thin
79	wrapper. Otherwise it's a project bridge consuming (S3.pi.1).
80	
81	Estimate: ~80–150 LOC. **Priority HIGH.**
82

85	**2. `Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite`** (S3.sep.2)
86	
87	```lean
88	/-- (S3.sep.2) A geometrically reduced finite field extension is
89	separable.
90	
91	Blueprint: `lem:S3_sep_2_geom_reduced_finite_field_ext_is_separable`.
92	Stacks Tag 0BJF. -/
93	theorem Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite
94	    {k F : Type u} [Field k] [Field F] [Algebra k F]
95	    [Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced k F] [FiniteDimensional k F] :
96	    Algebra.IsSeparable k F := by
97	  sorry
98	```
99	
100	Proof recipe: `F ⊗_k \bar k` reduced + finite-dim ⇒ Artinian product
101	of fields; dimension count `dim_{\bar k} (F ⊗_k \bar k) = [F : k]`;
102	the number of distinct `k`-embeddings `F ↪ \bar k` equals `[F : k]`
103	exactly when `F / k` is separable.
104	
105	If Mathlib already proves this in the `IsSeparable` family (verify
106	via `lean_leansearch` "geometrically reduced separable"), the lemma
107	is a one-liner.
108	
109	Estimate: ~30–50 LOC.

113	**3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Gamma_baseChange_iso_tensor_of_proper`** (S3.pi.1)
114	
115	```lean
116	/-- (S3.pi.1) For a proper scheme `X` over a field `k` and a field
117	extension `K/k`, the canonical map
118	`Γ(X, O_X) ⊗_k K → Γ(X_K, O_{X_K})` is an isomorphism.
119	
120	Blueprint: `lem:S3_pi_1_Gamma_baseChange_iso_tensor_of_proper`.
121	Stacks Tag 02KH (specialised to H^0). -/
122	theorem Gamma_baseChange_iso_tensor_of_proper
123	    {k K : Type u} [Field k] [Field K] [Algebra k K]
124	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [X.Over (Spec (.of k))]
125	    [IsProper (X ↘ Spec (.of k))] :
126	    -- the canonical map / iso (prover picks the natural Mathlib idiom)
127	    sorry := by
128	  sorry
129	```
130	
131	This is the DEEP one. The prover may legitimately PARTIAL with a
132	structured `sorry` documenting the closure path:
133	- Cover `X` by finitely many affines `U_i = Spec A_i` (quasi-compactness
134	  from properness).
135	- Čech complex computes `Γ(X) = eq(∏_i A_i ⇒ ∏_{i,j} A_{ij})`.
136	- Tensor with `K` over `k` preserves the equalizer (flatness of `K/k`).
137	- For affines: `(A ⊗_k K) ≅ Γ(U_K, O_{U_K})` (Stacks Tag 00DS; verified
138	  in Mathlib via `pullbackSpecIso` / `Spec_baseChange` family).
139	- Reassemble.
140	
141	Estimate: ~150–250 LOC. **Priority MEDIUM.** PARTIAL acceptable —
142	this sub-claim's residual `sorry` then propagates upward to substep 3
143	of `constants_integral_over_base_field` (Lane 2 below), but the build
144	still compiles.

148	**4. `Algebra.IsPurelyInseparable.of_unique_minPrime_baseChange`** (S3.pi.2)
149	
150	```lean
151	/-- (S3.pi.2) A finite-dimensional field extension `F / k` is purely
152	inseparable when `F ⊗_k \bar k` has a unique minimal prime.
153	
154	Blueprint: `lem:S3_pi_2_isPurelyInseparable_of_unique_minPrime_baseChange`.
155	Stacks Tag 05DH. -/
156	theorem Algebra.IsPurelyInseparable.of_unique_minPrime_baseChange
157	    {k F : Type u} [Field k] [Field F] [Algebra k F]
158	    [FiniteDimensional k F]
159	    (hUnique : ∀ p q : Ideal (TensorProduct k F (AlgebraicClosure k)),
160	        p.IsPrime → q.IsPrime → p ≤ q → p = q ∨ q = ⊤) :
161	    -- the prover may pick the natural Mathlib idiom for "unique minimal
162	    -- prime"; the body works through the prime-ideal lattice
163	    IsPurelyInseparable k F := by
164	  sorry
165	```
166	
167	Proof recipe: `F ⊗_k \bar k` is a finite-dim `\bar k`-algebra.
168	Quotient by nilradical: a domain finite over `\bar k`, hence a field
169	(Artinian + integral domain). Field finite over algebraically closed
170	`\bar k` is `\bar k` itself. So `(F ⊗_k \bar k) / Nil ≅ \bar k`,
171	and `[F : k]` is the rank, with all geometric points collapsed. By
172	Stacks Tag 05DH, `F / k` is purely inseparable.
173	
174	Estimate: ~50–100 LOC.

32	## KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero (L168 → L123 after edits)
33	
34	### Attempt 1
35	
36	- **Approach:** Signature inflation per planner (BR.1) — added `[CharZero k]`

40	    extract `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth k B` (note: the relative-dimension
41	    class is a `Prop`, not registered as an instance, so dispatch through
42	    the named theorem).
43	  - `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` to get
44	    `Module.Free B Ω[B⁄k]`.
45	  - `Module.Free.chooseBasis B Ω[B⁄k]` to pick a basis.
46	  - `(basis.coord i).compDer (KaehlerDifferential.D k B) : Derivation k B B`
47	    to extract the i-th coordinate derivation (BR.3).
48	  - `_hCoordVanish : ∀ i, ((basis.coord i).compDer D) b = 0` proved by

57	  is positioned for a SINGLE derivation `b' : B → B`, not for the
58	  INTERSECTION of multiple coordinate derivation kernels. The kernel of
59	  one `∂_i` alone is strictly larger than `range (algebraMap k B)` (e.g.
60	  `B = k[x_1, x_2]`, `∂_1 = ∂/∂x_1`, `ker ∂_1 = k[x_2] ⊋ k`). The (BR.5)
61	  joint-kernel collapse therefore requires a coordinate-induction
62	  argument on the standard-smooth presentation
63	  `B ≅ k[x_1, …, x_n, y_1, …, y_m] / (f_1, …, f_m)` with invertible
64	  Jacobian — substantive ~40–80 LOC Mathlib-PR-grade iter-150+ project work.
65	- **Next step:** Iter-150+ project lemma implementing
66	  `KaehlerDifferential.coordinateDeriv_jointKernel_collapse_of_charZero`

222	## Knowledge Base
223	
224	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
225	
226	- **Smart-proof reduction `Function.Surjective (algebraMap k Γ) ⟸ IsPurelyInseparable k Γ ∧ Algebra.IsSeparable k Γ` via `IsPurelyInseparable.surjective_algebraMap_of_isSeparable`**
227	  *(NEW iter-148 Archon canonical — surfaced by the iter-148 prover lane on `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean` `constants_integral_over_base_field` substep (3).)*
228	  When the goal is `Function.Surjective (algebraMap k F)` for a finite field extension `F/k` and the route through "explicit algebraic closure base change + flat-base-change-of-Γ" hits a Mathlib gap (e.g. proper-Γ-flat-base-change in Mathlib b80f227), the **smart-proof reduction** to the conjunction `IsPurelyInseparable k F ∧ Algebra.IsSeparable k F` closes via Mathlib's `IsPurelyInseparable.surjective_algebraMap_of_isSeparable` (`Mathlib/FieldTheory/PurelyInseparable/Basic.lean:158`). The reduction **does not eliminate the Mathlib gap**: it relocates the residual into the two sub-claims `IsPurelyInseparable k F` (from `GeometricallyIrreducible` + base change ⇒ unique min prime) and `Algebra.IsSeparable k F` (from smoothness ⇒ geom-reduced ⇒ separable). The advantage is that each sub-claim is independently named at sub-decomposition granularity. **Iter-148 instance** on `constants_integral_over_base_field`: the goal `Function.Surjective ((X ↘ Spec(.of k)).appTop.hom)` was reduced via `rw [RingHom.range_eq_top]` + the algebraization chain `Scheme.ΓSpecIso.inv ≫ appTop` + `RingHom.toAlgebra` to `Function.Surjective (algebraMap k Γ(X, ⊤))` and then to the named conjunction. Reusable any time a `Function.Surjective (algebraMap k F)` claim can be approached via "PI + Sep" (typically: geometric irreducibility provides PI from base change, and smoothness provides Sep from geom-reducedness).
229	
230	- **`Scheme.ΓSpecIso` (NOT bare `ΓSpecIso`) — fully-qualified namespace required**
231	  *(NEW iter-148 Archon canonical — surfaced by the iter-148 prover lane on `constants_integral_over_base_field`.)*
232	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ΓSpecIso (R : CommRingCat) : Γ(Spec R, ⊤) ≅ R` (Mathlib `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Scheme.lean:606`) is inside the `Scheme` namespace; the bare identifier `ΓSpecIso` does NOT resolve unless `open Scheme` is in effect. Reusable any time the project needs to algebraize the canonical iso `Γ(Spec R, ⊤) ≅ R`. **Iter-148 instance**: the `set α := (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (.of k)).inv ≫ (X ↘ Spec (.of k)).appTop` line at `ChartAlgebra.lean:313` initially failed with `Unknown identifier 'ΓSpecIso'` (3 sites); fix is to fully-qualify to `Scheme.ΓSpecIso`.
233	
234	### Anti-Patterns (do not retry)
235	
236	- **Anti-pattern: ship a `theorem foo : P := by sorry` where the in-source docstring openly admits `P` is mathematically false under stated hypotheses (the "honest-wrong-signature" pattern)**
237	  *(NEW iter-148 Archon canonical — surfaced by `lean-auditor-iter148` flagging `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero` at `ChartAlgebra.lean:123` as the "weakened-wrong-definition" anti-pattern in its purest form.)*
238	  When a Lean declaration's body is `sorry` and the surrounding docstring openly admits the conclusion is FALSE under the stated hypotheses (iter-148 instance: `b ∈ (algebraMap k B).range` fails for `B = k[X]`, `b = X^p` in characteristic `p`, with `[Field k] [CommRing B] [Algebra k B] [Algebra.FiniteType k B] (hDb : D b = 0)`), the declaration cannot be closed honestly without changing the signature. **Honesty about wrongness does not make the claim provable.** The pattern is morally equivalent to an excuse-comment (`-- TODO: replace`, `-- placeholder`) even when the docstring frames the wrongness as "the blueprint-mandated entry-point name" — it documents *intentionally shipping a wrong signature with a permanent sorry*. **Rule**: if the docstring would need to admit the conclusion is FALSE under stated hypotheses, the signature must be inflated before the body can be filled — OR the blueprint anchor name must migrate to a renamed lemma that is actually provable. The plan agent's "the consumer expects this name" framing is not sufficient ground to ship the wrong signature; the cost is a permanent invisible sorry that no future prover lane can discharge. **Iter-148 corrective path** for the KDM case: inflate to `[CharZero k] + [Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension k B]` (and propagate to β-core consumer), or commit to (p1) char-p Cartier chain with `[Algebra.IsStandardSmooth k B]` + Frobenius hypothesis. Documented at `recommendations.md` (iter-148 review) § "CRITICAL — KDM signature".

240	- **Anti-pattern: carry decorative typeclasses on a consumer that does not invoke its callee with them (the "consumer-with-unused-typeclasses" pattern)**
241	  *(NEW iter-148 Archon canonical — surfaced by `lean-auditor-iter148` + `lean-vs-blueprint-checker-chartalgebra-iter148` on `df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` at `ChartAlgebra.lean:196`.)*
242	  When a consumer carries four `C`-side scheme typeclasses (`[Smooth (C ↘ Spec k)]`, `[IsProper]`, `[IsReduced]`, `[GeometricallyIrreducible]`) and the body is a one-line wrapper around a helper that accepts none of them, the typeclasses are **decorative**. The signature *looks* like it has more chart-of-proper-curve context, but typeclass synthesis cannot bridge them through the helper call. Either (a) prune the unused typeclasses to align the signature with the actual body's commitment, or (b) inflate the helper's signature to consume them substantively. Maintaining the decorative typeclasses is the visual analog of the wrong-signature anti-pattern above — the consumer reads as if it does more than its body actually does. **Rule**: every typeclass / hypothesis in a signature should be consumed somewhere in the body; if none of the body's term-level identifiers consume the typeclass, prune it or strengthen the body. **Iter-148 instance**: `ChartAlgebra.lean:196 GrpObj.df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` carries `[Smooth (C ↘ Spec k)] [IsProper] [IsReduced] [GeometricallyIrreducible]` on `C` but the body is `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero hDb` which consumes nothing from `C`. The iter-148 review-phase `% NOTE:` block on the blueprint side authorises the iter-148 thin-wrapper disposition pending iter-149+ substantive refinement; the Lean-side fix is queued as REC-3-bis. Reusable as a flag for any "consumer-of-named-helper" closure where the consumer's signature is wider than the helper's.

Requirements: (a) the consumer's hypotheses are exactly the standing premises the helper consumes internally; (b) the helper exists by name (even if its body is `sorry`); (c) namespace ordering lets the consumer reference the helper (iter-147 instance required re-ordering KDM to land **before** the second `namespace GrpObj` reopening so β-core could reference `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero`). 123	theorem KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero
124	    {k : Type u} [Field k] [CharZero k]
125	    {B : Type u} [CommRing B] [Algebra k B] [Algebra.FiniteType k B]
126	    {n : ℕ} [Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n k B]
127	    {b : B} (hDb : _root_.KaehlerDifferential.D k B b = 0) :
128	    b ∈ (algebraMap k B).range := by
129	  -- Reverse inclusion (recorded for downstream symmetry):
130	  -- `D : Derivation k B Ω[B⁄k]` vanishes on the algebraMap image by
131	  -- `Derivation.map_algebraMap`.
132	  have _hRev : ∀ a : k, _root_.KaehlerDifferential.D k B (algebraMap k B a) = 0 :=
133	    fun a => (_root_.KaehlerDifferential.D k B).map_algebraMap a
134	  -- (BR.2) Free-module structure on `Ω[B⁄k]` from the standard-smooth
135	  -- chart hypothesis: `Module.Free B Ω[B⁄k]` of rank `n`. The relative-
136	  -- dimension class is a `Prop`, not registered as an instance for
137	  -- `IsStandardSmooth`, so dispatch through the named theorem.
138	  haveI _hStdSm : Algebra.IsStandardSmooth k B :=
139	    Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth n
140	  haveI _hFree : Module.Free B (Ω[B⁄k]) :=
141	    Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential
142	  -- (BR.3) Coordinate-derivation construction. For a chosen basis
143	  -- `{dx_i}_{i : ChooseBasisIndex B Ω[B⁄k]}` of `Ω[B⁄k]`, each B-linear
144	  -- coordinate functional `basis.coord i : Ω[B⁄k] →ₗ[B] B` composes with
145	  -- the universal Kähler derivation `D : Derivation k B Ω[B⁄k]` (via
146	  -- `LinearMap.compDer`) to produce a k-derivation
147	  -- `∂_i : Derivation k B B`. Since `D b = 0` (hypothesis), each
148	  -- `∂_i b = (basis.coord i) (D b) = (basis.coord i) 0 = 0` (record
149	  -- this for the (BR.5) consumer).
150	  let _basis := Module.Free.chooseBasis B (Ω[B⁄k])
151	  have _hCoordVanish : ∀ i, ((_basis.coord i).compDer
152	      (_root_.KaehlerDifferential.D k B)) b = 0 := by
153	    intro i
154	    -- `∂_i b = (basis.coord i) (D b) = (basis.coord i) 0 = 0` via the
155	    -- `LinearMap.compDer` coe-unfolding and `LinearMap.map_zero`.
156	    simp [Derivation.coe_comp, hDb]
157	  -- (BR.4) `Differential B` instance: each `∂_i : Derivation k B B`
158	  -- restricts to a `Derivation ℤ B B` via `Derivation.restrictScalars`,
159	  -- which is the witness for the `Differential B` structure. Multiple
160	  -- coordinate `Differential B` instances are mutually incompatible
161	  -- (Lean's typeclass synthesis picks one); we keep this as an
162	  -- explicit `let` rather than registering it globally.
163	  --
164	  -- (BR.5) JOINT-KERNEL COLLAPSE — the substantive remaining gap.
165	  -- Mathlib's `Differential.ContainConstants A B` typeclass packages
166	  -- the property "`b' = 0 ⇒ b ∈ range (algebraMap A B)`" for a SINGLE
167	  -- derivation `b' : B → B`. For our setup, `∂_1 b = 0` alone is
168	  -- INSUFFICIENT — the kernel of a single coordinate derivation is
169	  -- strictly larger than `range (algebraMap k B)`. For instance, in
170	  -- `B = k[x_1, x_2]` with `∂_1 = ∂/∂x_1`, `ker ∂_1 = k[x_2] ⊋ k`.
171	  --

173	  -- kernel `⋂_i ker(∂_i) = range (algebraMap k B)`. The argument
174	  -- structure: induct on the standard-smooth presentation

176	  -- Jacobian `det(∂f_i/∂y_j)`; the Jacobian condition expresses each

244	/-- Integrally-closed-constants helper: in a (smooth) proper geometrically
245	irreducible scheme `X` over a base field `k`, the global sections
246	`Γ(X, O_X)` are exhausted by the image of the structure-morphism's
247	action on the top open, i.e.\ the `appTop` ring hom of the structure
248	morphism `X ⟶ Spec k` is surjective. Equivalently, `Γ(X, O_X) ≅ k` as
249	`k`-algebras (modulo the canonical iso `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≃ k`).
250	
251	Blueprint: `chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` §
252	`\lem:constants_integral_over_base_field`. Three-substep recipe (per
253	the iter-146 prover-lane reading):
254	(1) `IsReduced X` + `GeometricallyIrreducible (X ↘ Spec k)` (over the
255	    singleton-base `Spec k`) ⇒ `IsIntegral X` (combine
256	    `GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton` with
257	    `isIntegral_of_irreducibleSpace_of_isReduced`).
258	(2) `IsIntegral X` + `UniversallyClosed (X ↘ Spec k)` (from
259	    `IsProper`) ⇒ `Γ(X, O_X)` is a field
260	    (`isField_of_universallyClosed`); and additionally
261	    `LocallyOfFiniteType (X ↘ Spec k)` ⇒ the `appTop` map is finite
262	    (`finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`). So `Γ(X, O_X)` is a finite
263	    field extension of `k`.
264	(3) Geometric irreducibility ⇒ this field extension is trivial via
265	    base change to `\bar k`: `Γ(X, O_X) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ Γ(X_{\bar k},
266	    ⊤) = \bar k` (the latter equality by the same chain applied to
267	    `X_{\bar k}` over `\bar k`, which is integral after base change
268	    because geometric irreducibility is stable under base change).
269	    A finite field extension `Γ` of `k` with `Γ ⊗_k \bar k = \bar k`
270	    has `dim_k Γ = 1`, hence `Γ = k`.

467	/-- Scheme-level lift: two morphisms `f, g : C ⟶ A` in `Over (Spec k)`
468	agreeing on a non-empty open subset `U ⊆ C.left` are equal, when `A`
469	is separated over `Spec k`, `C.left` is reduced, and `C` is
470	geometrically irreducible over `Spec k`.
471	
472	This is a thin renaming of the iter-125 packaging
473	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` (in
474	`AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean`) under the
475	`Scheme.Over.ext_of_diff_zero` name committed by the iter-145
476	chart-algebra planner. Per the blueprint's three-step
477	proof recipe (`chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` §
478	`\lem:Scheme_Over_ext_of_diff_zero`), the iter-125 packaging
479	discharges Step 3 (the `ext_of_eqOnOpen` packaging) directly given the
480	`eqOnOpen` hypothesis. Iter-147+ will refine the signature to *also*
481	take a chart-algebra `df = dg` hypothesis and *derive* `eqOnOpen` from
482	it via Steps 1–2 of the recipe (chart-algebra
483	`df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` β-core; currently a
484	deferred sub-piece, see L97 above). The lift here is the iter-146
485	sorry-free closure under the planner's chart-algebra envelope; the
486	substantive Steps 1–2 derivation lands once the β-core sub-piece
487	closes.
488	
489	Hypotheses match `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`; the renaming preserves
490	the iter-125 proof discipline that `IsSeparated A.hom` (not the
491	stronger `IsProper`) is sufficient for the rigidity packaging. -/
492	theorem ext_of_diff_zero
493	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
494	    {C A : Over (Spec (.of k))}
495	    [IsSeparated A.hom]
496	    [IsReduced C.left]
497	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
498	    (f g : C ⟶ A) (U : C.left.Opens) (hU : (U : Set C.left).Nonempty)
499	    (hUf : (U.ι : (U : C.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ C.left) ≫ f.left =
500	      (U.ι : (U : C.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ C.left) ≫ g.left) :
501	    f = g :=
502	  AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen f g U hU hUf
503	
504	end Over
505	
506	end Scheme
507	
508	end AlgebraicGeometry

(all four are PARTIAL scaffolding); KDM (p2) bridge body L194 is
still a structured `sorry` (BR.5 joint-kernel collapse).

   mode** with the H1Cotangent-vanishing alternative as the

   with concrete decomposed sub-claims) or pivoting to H1Cotangent
   vanishing. Silently scheduling another path (b) prover round

If the cross-domain consult returns "stay on path (b)", a
prover round on the tractable closers ((S3.sep.2) + (S3.pi.2);
each ~50–80 LOC) is the lowest-risk closure-target for
iter-150.

## HIGH PRIORITY (priority-ordered)

### Path (b) closer-targets (tractable iter-150 prover candidates)

1. **(S3.sep.2)** `Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite`
   (`ChartAlgebraS3.lean:199`). Mathlib bridges identified:
   `Field.finSepDegree_eq_finrank_iff`,

   (`ChartAlgebraS3.lean:269`). Mathlib bridge:

of path (b) and lets the iter-150 review confirm the hSep branch
closure end-to-end (since Lane 1 sorries propagate up through the

`ChartAlgebra.lean:194` — joint-kernel collapse of coordinate
derivations on the standard-smooth presentation

### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)

- **Smart-proof reduction `Function.Surjective (algebraMap k Γ) ⟸ IsPurelyInseparable k Γ ∧ Algebra.IsSeparable k Γ` via `IsPurelyInseparable.surjective_algebraMap_of_isSeparable`**
input.new_string:
### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)

- **`FiniteDimensional k Γ` bridge from `((appTop).hom).Finite` via `Iso.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv` + `RingEquiv.finite` + `RingHom.Finite.comp` + `RingHom.finite_algebraMap.mp`**
  *(NEW iter-149 Archon canonical — surfaced by the iter-149 Lane 2 prover lane closing the `constants_integral_over_base_field` hSep branch project-internally.)*
  Mathlib b80f227 gives `((X ↘ Spec k).appTop.hom).Finite` via `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` for proper `X` over a field `k`; converting this to `FiniteDimensional k Γ(X, ⊤)` (needed by `Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite` and other finite-field-extension consumers) requires a 4-step Mathlib chain. **Recipe**: (1) extract `RingEquiv` from `Scheme.ΓSpecIso (.of k)` via `CategoryTheory.Iso.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv` (declared in `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Ring.Basic`); (2) get `((Scheme.ΓSpecIso (.of k)).inv.hom).Finite` via `RingEquiv.symm.finite` (declared in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic`); (3) compose with `_hAppTopFinite` via `RingHom.Finite.comp` to get `(α.hom).Finite` where `α = (ΓSpecIso).inv ≫ (appTop)`; (4) bridge `α.hom.Finite ↔ (algebraMap k Γ).Finite` definitionally via `α.hom.toAlgebra` then close via `RingHom.finite_algebraMap.mp` (declared in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Defs`). **Iter-149 instance**: at `ChartAlgebra.lean:445–455` the chain produces `Module.Finite k ↥(X.presheaf.obj (Opposite.op ⊤))` from `_hAppTopFinite : ((X ↘ Spec(.of k)).appTop.hom).Finite`. Reusable any time a scheme-side appTop-finiteness witness needs to flow to module-side finiteness over the base field. The chain is also expected to be reusable in the hPI branch closure (iter-150+) — step (v) of the base-change chain needs `FiniteDimensional k Γ` for `(S3.pi.2)`.

- **First-class sub-claim decomposition as a strategy unit (one consolidated sorry → multiple named sub-claims with documented Mathlib bridges)**
  *(NEW iter-149 Archon canonical — surfaced by the iter-149 progress-critic CHURNING corrective + the iter-149 blueprint-writer's `(S3.sep.1) / (S3.sep.2) / (S3.pi.1) / (S3.pi.2)` promotion.)*
  When a sorry has accumulated PARTIAL verdicts across K=3 iters with consistent blocker phrasing, the iter-N progress-critic CHURNING verdict mandates **structural decomposition** as the primary corrective. **Recipe**: (a) blueprint-writer promotes the conjunction-style sub-claims to first-class `\begin{lemma}` blocks with `\label{}`/`\lean{}`/`\uses{}` + proof sketches + Stacks Tag citations; (b) the prover lane scaffolds the new declarations into a new file (or a new section of an existing file) with correct signatures + structured `sorry` bodies + documented closure paths inline; (c) the consumer site is refactored to consume the new named lemmas (in the iter-149 instance, the consolidated `⟨hPI, hSep⟩` sorry was split into named branches via `refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩`). **Trade-off**: strict sorry count INCREASES (one consolidated wrapper → multiple first-class sorries; iter-149: 5 → 9), but each new sorry is independently-targetable with named Mathlib bridges; subsequent iters close each separately. **Iter-149 instance**: `constants_integral_over_base_field` substep 3 / KDM bridge ⇒ `(S3.sep.1)` + `(S3.sep.2)` + `(S3.pi.1)` + `(S3.pi.2)` named first-class decls + `(BR.1)`–`(BR.5)` named sub-steps for the KDM (p2) bridge. The decomposition is planner-deliberate and the strict-count metric should not be read in isolation against it.

- **`Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n k B` is a `Prop` not registered as an instance; dispatch through the named theorem `.isStandardSmooth n` to extract `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth k B`**
  *(NEW iter-149 Archon canonical — surfaced by the iter-149 Lane 2 prover lane on `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero` (BR.2).)*
  Mathlib b80f227 ships `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension : ℕ → (k : Type u) → (B : Type u) → [CommRing k] → [CommRing B] → [Algebra k B] → Prop` as a `Prop`, not as a typeclass instance source. Code that needs to consume `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth k B` (e.g. `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` for `Module.Free B (Ω[B⁄k])`) must dispatch through the named theorem `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth n`. **Iter-149 instance** at `ChartAlgebra.lean:138–139`: `haveI _hStdSm : Algebra.IsStandardSmooth k B := Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth n`. Also: when declaring `{n : ℕ}` as an explicit binder, downstream call sites must pass `(n := n)` (iter-149 instance at L240 in the `df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` consumer; failing to do so produces `typeclass instance problem is stuck — Lean will not try to resolve because the first type argument to Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension was a metavariable`). Reusable any time a `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth`-family typeclass needs to be unpacked from a relative-dimension hypothesis.

- **Smart-proof reduction `Function.Surjective (algebraMap k Γ) ⟸ IsPurelyInseparable k Γ ∧ Algebra.IsSeparable k Γ` via `IsPurelyInseparable.surjective_algebraMap_of_isSeparable`**

- **KDM (BR.5) joint-kernel collapse: `Differential.ContainConstants A B` covers single-derivation case; the joint kernel `⋂_i ker(∂_i) = range (algebraMap k B)` is the genuine project work (Stacks 07F4 + char-0 collapse)** *(NEW iter-149 Archon, do-not-retry-without-joint-kernel-helper)*: iter-149 Lane 2 closed (BR.2)–(BR.4) but (BR.5) joint-kernel collapse remains a structured `sorry` at `ChartAlgebra.lean:194`. **Do not retry single-derivation `Differential.ContainConstants` instances** on `B = k[x_1, ..., x_n]` / standard-smooth in the hope of collapsing the joint kernel — the kernel of one coordinate derivation `∂_i` alone is strictly larger than `range (algebraMap k B)` (counter-example: in `B = k[x_1, x_2]` with `∂_1 = ∂/∂x_1`, `ker ∂_1 = k[x_2] ⊋ k`). The collapse requires a coordinate induction on the standard-smooth presentation `B ≅ k[x_1, …, x_n, y_1, …, y_m] / (f_1, …, f_m)` with invertible Jacobian — substantive ~40–80 LOC Mathlib-PR-grade work. **Iter-150+ closure target**: implement `KaehlerDifferential.coordinateDeriv_jointKernel_collapse_of_charZero` (suggested name) in the project's `RingTheory/Derivation/` namespace (or as an in-tree `ChartAlgebra` helper) via the coordinate induction. Documented inline at `ChartAlgebra.lean:164–193`.

- **Mathlib b80f227 `Smooth ⇒ GeometricallyReduced (scheme morphism class)` is absent; verified by iter-149 Lane 1 prover lane full grep** *(NEW iter-149 Archon, do-not-retry-this-Mathlib-grep)*: iter-149 prover-lane full grep across `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth`, `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.SmoothFiber`, `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Geometrically.Reduced` confirms no instance bridging the scheme-side smooth-morphism class to the geometrically-reduced morphism class. Stacks Tag 04QM is the upstream anchor. **Do not re-dispatch `mathlib-analogist` on this specific question** — the gap is well-characterised. Iter-150+ closure paths: (a) build the morphism-class bridge in-tree (~80–150 LOC, Mathlib-PR-grade), or (b) short-circuit `(S3.sep.1) isGeometricallyReduced_Gamma_of_smooth` by proving `Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced k Γ` directly via the (S3.pi.1)-produced `Γ ⊗_k K ≅ Γ(X_K)` chain without routing through the morphism class. See iter/iter-149/review.md "Per-target outcome — Lane 1, (S3.sep.1)".

- **Iter-150 escalation hook FIRES (per iter-149 plan Decision 3): closure-route pivot conversation is mandatory** *(NEW iter-149 Archon, structural-escalation-hook)*: iter-149 Lane 1 closed 0 of 4 (S3.\*) sub-claims AND iter-149 Lane 2 KDM (p2) bridge body remains a structured `sorry` ((BR.5) joint-kernel collapse). Per the iter-149 plan agent's commitment, iter-150 MUST dispatch `mathlib-analogist` in `cross-domain-inspiration` mode on the H1Cotangent-vanishing reformulation as the candidate route-pivot, AND the iter-150 plan phase must explicitly commit to either continuing path (b) with the now-decomposed targets or pivoting to H1Cotangent vanishing. **Silently scheduling another path (b) prover round without the cross-domain consult is a missed escalation** that the next iter's progress-critic will flag. The hSep branch closure + KDM (BR.2)–(BR.4) advance are substantive intermediate progress and should weight the iter-150 plan agent's pivot-vs-stay decision, but the analogist consult itself is non-optional.

- **`KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero` (ChartAlgebra.lean:123) signature is mathematically wrong under stated hypotheses** *(NEW iter-148 Archon, do-not-retry-without-signature-fix; RESOLVED iter-149 via signature inflation to `[CharZero k]` + `[Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n k B]`)*
